I am kind of thinking ceasefire meant "sees fire"

#oott
Latest podcast from Sparta is out! #oott

Quoted in @TIME today:

“I don’t think we’re going to see anything, at least for the next year, at the very least,” June Goh, senior oil market analyst for Sparta Commodities, says, when asked when the price of crude oil could go back to pre-war levels around $75 a barrel. 

Goh says the war has taken 10 to 11 million barrels of crude oil offline per day, and the inventory drawn down to manage the situation will need to be replenished. High demand caused by replenishing the lost oil stock will keep prices elevated, she said.

https://t.co/Sr39oZASzW

I realize that my tone has been very pessimistic about the whole crisis.

So let me share my views on some positive outcomes that should emerge from this - and I would love to hear more good stuff from you guys too!

1⃣ Renewables will be a bigger part of our energy mix.  
 
2⃣Dependence on oil and derived products (like plastic) will reduce. 

3⃣ Refineries will stop closing and perhaps we can see a renaissance in refining within some countries!

4⃣ The world understands oil trading a bit better. 

Now it's your turn to share your half-full glasses! ⭐️

#oott

🗓️ Trump on April 9th:

"... Very soon, crude will beging moving..."

🗓️ Trump on March 3rd:

"... No matter what, the United States will ensure the free flow of energy..."

The physical Brent market remains very, very strong, even if Dated Brent has come off from its all-time high reached on Tuesday (down around $15). Physical premia for prompt oil (or as my colleague @AlaricN calls it: “ASAP barrels”) are sky high, >$20 above Dated Brent.

Both Tehran and Washington are upping the ante ahead of the ceasefire talks over the weekend. But raising the stakes  increases the miscalculation risk:

“We will definitely bring the management of the Strait of Hormuz to a new stage,” Iranian Supreme Leader Khamenei said.

Saudi Arabia details the impact of Iranian attacks on its facilities, saying it lost 600,000 b/d of production capacity (split between Manifa and Khurais fields). Plus hits against refineries: Satorp in Jubail, Ras Tanura, Samref  in Yanbu, and Riyadh. 

https://t.co/65TBSzptxa

Plus an attack on the Saudi East-West pipeline led to a loss of approximately 700,000 barrels per day in throughput (unclear whether that capacity has been lost completely or could be recovered using other pumping stations)
What’s not clear to me is when the UAE says that “… the era of courtesies has passed, and frankness has become a necessity…” is it talking toward Tehran — or Washington. Or maybe both?

In the middle of what some have described as the worst energy crisis ever (worst than 1973+1979+2022 combined), let me tell you that US natural gas prices are falling toward a 18-month low of $2.6 per mBtu. 

(And yes, North America gas market is an island, but it does matter)
*ISRAEL SAYS AGREES TO DIRECT TALKS WITH LEBANON

Japan updates on its oil situation 

(And it looks better that I had expected; but note that the country is signalling continues use of its SPR)

https://t.co/I7LTsMKX4o

UAE senior oil official Sultan Al Jaber denounces the Strait of Hormuz remains closed. “Access is being restricted, conditioned and controlled,” 

“Conditional passage is not passage. It is control by another name,” he says.

https://t.co/TFczKBO4kk

So now, this creates a bit of a problem for the White House, which as yesterday night was still pushed the ‘magic realism’ narrative that said the strait was opening, with a rising number of ships crossing. Effectively, the UAE says, on the record, that’s not true.

The good news: attacks have come down markedly (ex Lebanon), and the fragile ceasefire seems to be holding.

The bad news: so far, almost no movement of ships in-and-out of the Strait of Hormuz. Several Chinese oil tankers have pre-positioned to cross, but they are still waiting.

Japan is considering releasing additional amount of oil from its strategic petroleum reserve (equal to about 20 days of imports) as a full shipping resumption from the Persian Gulf remains uncertain, according to Kyodo News https://t.co/rqeZP4zw8m

INVESTMENT MANAGERS trimmed their bullish position in Europe’s benchmark Dutch TTF gas contract last week for the first time since the war between the United States and Iran started. Fund managers sold the equivalent of 14 terawatt-hours (TWh) of futures and options over the seven days ending on April 2 after purchasing a total of 214 TWh over the previous six weeks:

Energy Aspects:

“VLCCs migrating from Hormuz to carry record Atlantic crude volumes to Asia; vessels won’t return to Middle East until at least June.”

Translation:

Even if the Strait of Hormuz opens this very second, tanker flows INTO the Strait won’t come in size until June.

Production shut-in of ~11 million b/d won’t change.
Highly recommend Ed’s thread here. Thank you for sharing with all of us your expertise.
Appreciate the explanation. Thank you!

Based on the latest tanker flow data, US commercial crude storage is expected to reach ~404 million bbls by the end of June, regardless of what happens to the Strait of Hormuz.

Our analysis also assumes 74.5 million bbls of SPR release into US commercial crude storage.

This will put us ~24 million bbls away from operational minimum.

It's funny to read "what can they do about it" on the Saudis and UAE not accepting a toll fee arrangement (for the Strait of Hormuz).

They can keep doing the bypass. If 4.7 million b/d remains offline, you will have the same crisis we have now.
Saudi and UAE can keep 4.7 million b/d shut-in.

Guys, it's not rocket science. You need empty tankers to go into the Persian Gulf to alleviate the production shut-in. Without tankers going in, the shut-in continues. That's ~11 million bbls a day. 

This is the Strait of Hormuz over the last 24 hours. 

Source: @MarineTraffic https://t.co/mfZ044wito
“Moving to a new stage in managing Strait of Hormuz” is a non-starter for Saudi, UAE, and Kuwait.

Friendly reminder to anyone trading $USO or $UCO.

$USO is now in June WTI contracts.

$UCO is in June, July, and December WTI contracts. https://t.co/CrqxFOFR6a

If you want to know when it’s time to get bearish oil, just watch refining margins. 

In COVID, some of the signals were:

First, these signals peaked. 

Floating storage

Tanker rates

Contango

After, refining margins, which were negative in early May started to rebound. 

Refining margins only rebounded because product storage started to drain. 

Refineries at the time were in a precarious place. OPEC+ had just reduced production by 10 million b/d. Mandatory lockdowns destroyed oil demand, so refining margins had to go up in order for crude oil to rally. 

When it finally did, that was when crude started to rally with a bit more leg. 

This time around, a similar playbook will happen but in reverse. 

Refining margins will be a key indicator for when the global economy cannot handle higher prices any longer. We are already seeing some of that in Southeast Asia, but it’s not widespread yet. 

Once you hear about how refineries are forced to cut throughput and can no longer afford crude, that will be the signal to sell your crude oil longs.
As with anything in the oil market, we will reach an “obvious” point. Just focus on the signals.

Shoutout to @PauloMacro for writing about this 2 days ago:

https://t.co/RmAGmWX54x

EA’s Amrita is saying ~13 million b/d of crude oil production is shut-in now. 

This would have to be the breakdown:

Iraq: -4 million b/d

Kuwait: -2.4 million b/d 

Bahrain: -0.18 million b/d

UAE: -1.8 million b/d

Saudi: -4.5 million b/d (?)

My base case assumed Saudis to shut-in 3 million b/d, but she noted “precautionary” shut-ins. 

So the figure could be as large as 4.5 million b/d. Either way, the shut-in is massive.

Great job @ea_amrita 

https://t.co/z92FsBXugU

One of my favourite things about Iran’s Hormuz tolls aspirations is that Trump can’t decide where he stands on the issue.

Some days he couldn’t care less, other days like today he claims it’s a dealbreaker, yesterday he was musing about splitting the toll proceeds with Tehran: https://t.co/HpkAY2L4fa

The vast majority of the “dislocation between physical and paper barrels” is very simply extreme backwardation, meaning the steep premiums for spot deliverable barrels  (as BBG referred to them today, ASAP barrels) relative to later-dated cargoes.

Crude futures curves have experienced record prompt backwardation (prompt premium to second month), and that steep backwardation keeps extending through to spot physical prices, which are even nearer term deliverable than prompt futures (WTI trading May atm, Brent trading June)
https://t.co/9gGIvxcDJw

[Kuwaiti] air defenses in the Armed Forces are currently dealing with hostile attacks from drones that penetrated the country's airspace, targeting a number of vital facilities.

Saudi Arabia confirms hits to the critical East-West pipeline (a pumping station), which led to a 700 kbpd throughput loss.

As well as confirmed strikes to upstream production assets (!!) that have reduced Saudi production *capacity* by 600 kbpd.

On top of downstream refining/processing attacks.

Official Saudi Press Agency:
Link: https://t.co/mh53GPCiZ5
I'm not sure who needs to hear this but Iran's 15-ship daily limit isn't going to be 15 VLCCs each laden with 2 million barrels of oil.

Saudi Arabia's East-West pipeline is the single most important rerouting offset to the Hormuz stoppage with ~7 MMbpd total capacity and enabling a ~5 MMbpd swing from Gulf flows to the Red Sea.

And now attacks on the pipeline are beginning to hamper flow on that critical offset.
*TRUMP SAYS IF IRAN DOESN'T MAKE A DEAL, IT WILL BE PAINFUL: NBC https://t.co/VLw2aFyjWR
Is the Strait of Hormuz open? https://t.co/GM0jdFCFAe

A ceasefire with still throttled (no more than 15 ships daily) Hormuz throughput well serves Tehran's strategic interest.

Iran's main leverage is a closed Strait + time, so building another 2 weeks of pressure on the global economy while not being bombed feels ideal from the Iranian perspective.

That spares the market some fear of further escalation (facility attacks, etc.) but at the price of the status quo stoppage getting worse.

It’s not open.

Global #container #freight rates recorded a sixth consecutive, albeit moderate, weekly increase, lifting the Drewry Composite Index to USD 2,309 per 40ft. box - a three-month high. 
During the past week, a divergence emerged, with rates to Europe declining while those from China to New York and Los Angeles continued to rise.
Overall, the index is up 4% year-to-date and 2% over the past twelve months.
#Crudeoil rebounds toward USD 100 as Hormuz bottlenecks keep physical market tight https://t.co/CMKXsEOzxn

#Commodities

Crude prices rebounded following their biggest one-day drop since April 2020, with Brent challenging resistance ahead of USD 100. The Strait of Hormuz remains effectively closed after Israeli attacks on Lebanon - seen by Iran as a breach of the fragile ceasefire. According to the WSJ, Iran has told mediators it will limit traffic through the Strait to around 12 ships per day and impose tolls under the ceasefire. That is a sharp reduction from the 100+ daily transits before the war, effectively leaving most of the 1,000+ commercial vessels inside the Persian Gulf stranded. At the same time, shipowners may hesitate to send in empty tankers to load fresh cargoes, further tightening near-term supply.

In the physical market, stress remains evident. Dated Brent - reflecting prompt cargoes - settled at USD 124.5 on Wednesday, with several bids left unanswered (Platts), underscoring a rapidly tightening supply backdrop.

The ceasefire announcement triggered sharp cross-commodity moves, leaving the Bloomberg Commodity Index (BCOM) down 4.6% on the day. Losses in crude, diesel, and gasoline, as well as weakness in wheat, corn, and sugar, outweighed gains in metals, led by silver and copper.

US #crudeoil stockpiles rose for a seventh consecutive week to their highest level since June 2023. However, exports are set to surge, with 68 empty tankers (Kpler via FT) currently heading to the US, up sharply from 24 in the week before the war began.

Cushing inventories also climbed to near the five-year average, helping explain why June WTI continues to trade at a notable discount to June Brent.

Meanwhile, #gasoline and #diesel inventories declined, driven by a combination of lower refinery runs and strong exports to a fuel-starved global market amid ongoing Middle East disruptions.

US crude production edged lower for a seventh straight week to 13.6 mb/d - excluding the January freeze, the lowest since September - with no clear signs yet that producers are responding to elevated prices.
